sentences.text: Hi!
Hi, Angela.
How are you?
I'm good, how are you?
Good!
Angela… both Angela… me and Angela McKinney are both sitting in the same office, so, she has her screen up and can see it, and can see, but we're operating from my speakers.
That makes sense.
How are you?
Good, how are you?
I'm good!
I'm excited to talk to you guys again.
It's been a while.
It has, and thank you for getting back together with us.
I'm sorry, I got all kinds of sidetracked, and I'm like, okay, I need a refresher.
Of course, yeah, no, absolutely.
… Hi, Angela.
speaker, but nice to see you again.
You do?
Thank you.
So I will go through it again, high level, but before we get… I forgot, I couldn't find the email again.
How many units do you guys have in the portfolio?
331 and another 120 on the way.
331… when you say 120 on the way, when will that be delivered?
… I… I am hoping not this… not this coming March, but the next.
Oh, gosh.
So it's gonna be a bit… we are just now….
31 for the point of this conversation.
Yes, yes.
Okay, great.
Okay, so I will take you through the tool.
Are you guys… is everything still manual?
Has anything changed on your end, I guess?
Like, the whole process of collecting competitive data, market surveys, is that all manual still?
Yep.
Okay, and pricing is still manual as well?
Yes.
Okay, great.
Alright, let me share my screen with you all.
Alrighty, so this should look relatively familiar.
So this would be your landing page for your two properties.
They would each get their own individual card, and then to click on the… or to view the analytics, you just select view.
And there's been some updates since you last saw it, so it might look a little different, but mostly the same.
So up here, this is going to be your main navigation to see all the different reports we have.
Obviously, the first report's going to be that market survey report, but you can think of it as high-level, floor plans, granular unit level information, so… Basically, biggest to smallest.
And then we do have, up to 5 years' worth of historical data baked in the app as well.
Wow.
Okay, that's new, right?
So, when we last spoke, it was… we had 3 years.
So, we have 4 years, but you can actually, … you can use the filters, and you can say all time, and you can expose the 5th year.
Okay.
have 4 years, but then you can access the 5 years of historical data, which is fantastic.
So that would be great for, like, seasonality trends and things like that.
So here at the top, you'll see Basic Market Survey.
You could either provide us with a list of the comps, or our system can suggest comps for you, or a combination of both.
You can filter the information based off of if you just want to look at a couple of your comps, or you can look at it all together, all preference.
And then, again, you can filter the information based off of different bedrooms, bathrooms, square footage, date available, things like that.
I do have… there's all these other metrics that are not currently toggled on, but you would have access to all these other metrics.
Actually, I'm gonna look at… Vacant units.

And then you can drill into each of these properties to see more about them, so their address, when they were built, how many units, and who's currently managing the property, and a link out to their website as well.
Just as a reminder, so ApartmentIQ, it's all public data, so it's all coming from property websites.
The information's updated every single day, so you can see that time stamped here in the cor- up in the corner, so updated August 28th.
So you can feel confident anytime you guys log in to make any sort of business decision, it's up to date.
Just curious, how many people will be… would be using the tool?
Probably just the two of us.
Okay, perfect.
And then you would have full control over the comps, as I mentioned.
So, typically, a customer provides us with the list of comps that they have.
We also can do, some comp matching for you, but you would have full access to this, so you can add and remove comps as often as you want.
Okay, the property would be here in the purple pin.
Comps that are currently in your comp set are these white pins.
And then black pins are other properties in the area that you might want to consider adding to your comp set.
So that's literally as simple as saying add comp, and then you'd refresh your screen, and it's instantly added along with the historical data as well.
Over here on the left-hand side, you'll… it's just a list view of that, so you have your property at the top, your current comps.
If you want to change any of these, you could just X them off, and then you could come down here and add any additional comps that you'd like.
If you don't see it in the list, you can also search for the property as well.
We do provide this comp score, so this is… this is just, basically, just basic guidance.

So, it's a computer-generated score, and it takes into account the distance or proximity to your property, the year built or renovated, how many units are in the property, and then we do some pricing and amenity matching as well, and our system essentially just spits out a score.
we say that you guys know your comps best, so sometimes these align, but this just gives you some basic guidance if you don't know the market that well.
That's how that score is derived.
Right.

So there's 5 sections within the market survey, so we did separate these sections, so this is just gonna be your property summary, so we're gonna give you, for instance, the year built, what class is a property, how many units, who's currently managing them, a link out to their Google reviews, that's new since you've last seen it, and then we will display any, … concessions that they're offering at a property, floor plan, or a unit level, so… we're displaying.
those?
well, so that's coming directly from the property website, so that's a pop-up ribbon, banner, mouse over, whatever it might be.
We're able to capture that.
down below, these are going to be your calculated metrics.
So just as a reminder, because we're tracking the unit-level data every single day, that's how we're able to gather this information, because there's no integrations, it's all public data.
So for instance, your available units, if Unit 123 is available today, but it leaves the website.
We would fall… it would fall under applications, and we do hold that under 7 days, and then if it… Stays off the website for those 7 days, it would move into a lease.
So that's how we're getting this information.
What's wonderful is you can drill into all this information, so you can actually see the 7 units that we're reporting as leased on the property.
Same with available, you can look at all of these, and you can see exactly what units are showing as available at the property.
And that's not just for your property, but that's across the board for any of your comps as well.
Down below there is our pricing and availability.
And this is broken out by floor plan, so this is just a quick reference.

You can take a look to see how your one-bedrooms are positioned within the comp set, where you guys are stacked, where your net effective rent is, or who's offering the highest concession, whatever it might be.
And you can filter these.
Oh, I have the property pinned right now, but you can filter them just so you can quickly see where your property is stacked in comparison to its comps.
Okay.
Just below that, we're capturing fees and deposits, so as long as they're on the property website.
So, just, a little caveat, so I mentioned about property websites being our source of truth.
Fees and deposits and amenity information.
We are going to both the property website, but as well as to an ILS, like an Apartments.
com.
Apartments.
com does a really good job at laying this information out.

So, like, in our software, we have the ability to hide units.
That are available.
But if we have 10 one-bedrooms, maybe we only want the general public to know about 3 of them, so there is a sense of urgency.
So when we hide them, they're not on our website, they're not on any ILSs.
How do you guys get around this?
So one of two ways.
So if they are truly hidden, if they are not in your system at all, like, a lot of times, clients will just push the data out so they're not being seen.
If the data's pushed out, we're capturing those.
However, if you just don't have them listed at all, let me actually show you, we have a limited data tag.
I'll show… let me find an example for you.

… I can find an example, and I can send it to you after.
Oh, right here.

If you hover over it, it gives you a little, …

It should give you… it basically tells you that we feel this property… we think that this property is hiding units, so essentially, take that with a grain of salt, that we're… we're showing you what we're capturing, but… and that's why we call it limited data, but just know that they might be hiding units.
This is very common with, like, a lease-up property.
At lease-ups, typically, they're not releasing all of their units at one time, they're usually only releasing a certain amount of units.
So if that's the case, it will say limited data.

I don't know why this isn't working, but it gives you a little blip as to what the reasoning would be, like, we believe that they're hiding units, or sometimes it's floor plan only data, but if you click on it, we will show you what they are displaying.
So we will show you exactly what we're capturing, but basically, like.
We do believe that this property has additional units, they're just not releasing them.

com, be like, only show 3 at a time.

com is a great example, that's why we're not going to Apartments.
com to get the information, because a lot of times, Apartments.
com can actually limit the amount of floor plans or units within a specific floor plan.

Correct.

And again, you can look at this over different time frames, so just, like, for pricing and… trends and how it's… how it… essentially those floor plans have been leased.

If… if you don't like the… all different kinds of floor plans, we do do a roll-up where we show you just all of the studios, the average of the studios within that property, so we break it out that way as well, so whichever your preference is.
Perfect.
Then we have all of the unit level information.
And that's the granular unit-level data.

And you can look at it as the property, or broken out by floor plan.

Fair enough.

We no longer have setup fees.
Okay.
And we do also allow for unlimited users.
So, if you wanted your on-site teams to be able to view this information, you could actually set them up with read-only access, so there's different permission levels.

we're… and… How long is the agreement for?
